items.prompt_md: In Prim's algorithm, what does the min-heap store as its key, in contrast to Dijkstra's heap?
options: The weight of a single edge connecting the tree to an unvisited node
The cumulative distance from a fixed source node
The number of hops used so far
The total weight of the tree built so far
items.answer: The weight of a single edge connecting the tree to an unvisited node
items.explanation_md: Dijkstra's key is a running total (distance-from-source + edge weight). Prim's key is just the weight of the one edge that would attach a new node to the tree already built.

items.prompt_md: What does the "cut property" guarantee that makes Prim's greedy choice provably optimal?
options: For any split of nodes into "in the tree" and "not yet in the tree," the cheapest edge crossing that boundary is always safe to include in some minimum spanning tree
Every edge in the graph must eventually be included in the MST
The heap will never contain more than V entries at once
Removing the most expensive edge from any cycle always shortens the total tree weight
items.answer: For any split of nodes into "in the tree" and "not yet in the tree," the cheapest edge crossing that boundary is always safe to include in some minimum spanning tree
items.explanation_md: The cut property is why greedily adding the cheapest crossing edge at every step is provably optimal, not just a good heuristic.

items.prompt_md: How does Kruskal's algorithm decide whether to add a candidate edge to the growing MST?
options: It processes edges in increasing weight order and adds an edge only if its two endpoints are in different Union-Find components
It processes edges in decreasing weight order and always adds the next edge
It picks edges at random until the tree is fully connected
It relaxes every edge for V - 1 rounds, like Bellman-Ford
items.answer: It processes edges in increasing weight order and adds an edge only if its two endpoints are in different Union-Find components
items.explanation_md: Sorting by weight and skipping any edge that would connect two nodes already in the same component (which would create a cycle) is exactly how Kruskal's builds a minimum spanning tree.

items.prompt_md: What is the general time complexity of Prim's algorithm implemented with a binary heap, on a graph with `V` vertices and `E` edges?
options: O((V + E) log V)
O(E)
O(V + E)
items.answer: O((V + E) log V)
items.explanation_md: Just like Dijkstra, every edge can push once onto the heap and every push/pop costs O(log V), giving O((V + E) log V) overall.

items.prompt_md: `min_cost_connect_points` treats every pair of the `n` points as a potential edge (a complete graph, so `E` is about `n^2`). What is the time complexity of the heap-based Prim's shown in the lesson on this input?
options: O(n log n)
O(n^2 log n)
items.answer: O(n^2 log n)
items.explanation_md: Prim's general bound is O((V + E) log V). On a complete graph E is O(V^2) = O(n^2), so the bound becomes O(n^2 log n).

items.prompt_md: What dominates the time complexity of Kruskal's algorithm on a graph with `E` edges?
options: Sorting the edges, giving O(E log E)
The Union-Find operations, giving O(E^2)
Running BFS from every node, giving O(V * E)
Nothing dominates; it runs in O(1)
items.answer: Sorting the edges, giving O(E log E)
items.explanation_md: Near-constant-time (inverse-Ackermann) Union-Find operations are dwarfed by the one-time cost of sorting all E edges by weight.

items.prompt_md: Why can't you just try every possible spanning tree and keep the cheapest one?
options: The number of spanning trees on n nodes grows as n^(n-2) (Cayley's formula) — exponential, while Prim/Kruskal find the optimum greedily in polynomial time
Spanning trees don't actually have to include every node
There is always exactly one spanning tree in any graph
Trying every spanning tree would miss the true minimum
items.answer: The number of spanning trees on n nodes grows as n^(n-2) (Cayley's formula) — exponential, while Prim/Kruskal find the optimum greedily in polynomial time
items.explanation_md: Brute force is exponential; the cut property is what lets Prim's and Kruskal's guarantee optimality without enumerating every tree.

items.prompt_md: A buggy "Prim's" implementation pushes `(dist_from_source[u] + w, v)` onto the heap instead of `(w, v)`. What's actually wrong with it?
options: It's silently computing Dijkstra's shortest-path tree instead of a minimum spanning tree — the two algorithms differ only in this one heap key
Nothing; the two heap keys always produce the same tree
It will crash because `dist_from_source` is undefined at every node
It makes Prim's algorithm run faster
items.answer: It's silently computing Dijkstra's shortest-path tree instead of a minimum spanning tree — the two algorithms differ only in this one heap key
items.explanation_md: Prim's and Dijkstra's share an identical pop-skip-finalize skeleton; swapping in a cumulative distance instead of a raw edge weight turns one algorithm into the other.

items.prompt_md: In Prim's algorithm, why must you check `if u in visited: continue` right after popping `(w, u)`?
options: The same node can be pushed multiple times (once per edge reaching it from the growing tree), so only the first, cheapest pop is the edge you actually want to keep
To make sure `w` is never negative
To ensure the heap never runs out of memory
Because Union-Find requires this exact check
items.answer: The same node can be pushed multiple times (once per edge reaching it from the growing tree), so only the first, cheapest pop is the edge you actually want to keep
items.explanation_md: Just like Dijkstra's stale-entry guard, this skips duplicate, more expensive heap entries for a node whose cheapest connecting edge was already finalized.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the heap-based Prim's algorithm on a graph with `V` vertices and `E` edges?
options: O(V + E)
O(log V)
items.answer: O(V + E)
items.explanation_md: The adjacency representation costs O(V + E), and the heap and visited set together add at most O(V + E) more, so the total stays O(V + E).

items.prompt_md: You must connect every point in a set with wires so all points are reachable, minimizing total wire length. No particular start or end point is specified. Which approach fits?
options: Push `(0, start)` and repeatedly pop the frontier's cheapest edge, adding its endpoint to the tree
Push `(0, start)` and repeatedly pop the smallest cumulative distance from `start`
BFS from any point, treating every edge as weight 1
Binary search on the total wire length
items.answer: Push `(0, start)` and repeatedly pop the frontier's cheapest edge, adding its endpoint to the tree
items.explanation_md: "Connect everything as cheaply as possible" with no fixed source or destination is the minimum-spanning-tree fingerprint.

items.prompt_md: An unweighted graph where you just need the fewest hops between two specific nodes. Which approach fits?
options: Plain BFS from the source
Prim's algorithm, treating every edge weight as 1
Kruskal's algorithm with Union-Find
Bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation
items.answer: Plain BFS from the source
items.explanation_md: There is no "connect everything cheaply" or weighted shortest-path requirement here, just fewest hops between two nodes — a plain BFS traversal answers that directly.

items.prompt_md: You're given an explicit, sparse edge list (not a complete graph of coordinates) and need the minimum cost to connect all nodes. Which approach fits best?
options: Kruskal's algorithm: sort edges by weight, add each one via Union-Find as long as it doesn't create a cycle
Prim's algorithm, scanning the full O(V^2) adjacency matrix every step
Dijkstra's algorithm
Plain BFS
items.answer: Kruskal's algorithm: sort edges by weight, add each one via Union-Find as long as it doesn't create a cycle
items.explanation_md: When the graph is already given as a sparse edge list, sorting edges and growing with Union-Find (Kruskal's) avoids scanning a dense adjacency structure Prim's needs.

items.prompt_md: You need the cheapest way to travel from a specific city A to a specific city B in a weighted graph — not to connect every city cheaply. Which approach fits?
options: Dijkstra's algorithm from A
Prim's algorithm
Kruskal's algorithm
Relax edges for k + 1 rounds regardless of any hop limit
items.answer: Dijkstra's algorithm from A
items.explanation_md: A single fixed source and a specific target (or all targets) is a shortest-path question, not a connect-everything question — that's Dijkstra's territory, not MST's.

items.prompt_md: You need to know whether an undirected graph is a single connected component — not the minimum cost of anything. Which approach fits?
options: Plain BFS/DFS from any node, checking if it reaches every other node
Kruskal's algorithm with edge weights
Prim's algorithm from an arbitrary node, summing edge weights
Bellman-Ford relaxation
items.answer: Plain BFS/DFS from any node, checking if it reaches every other node
items.explanation_md: A yes/no connectivity question needs nothing more than a traversal; pulling in a weighted MST algorithm answers a question that was never asked.

items.prompt_md: You're given a plain list of edge weights and need the k-th smallest value quickly. There's no graph traversal or connectivity requirement at all. Which approach fits?
options: A heap (or quickselect) over the raw weight values
Kruskal's algorithm with Union-Find
Prim's algorithm from an arbitrary node
Dijkstra's algorithm
items.answer: A heap (or quickselect) over the raw weight values
items.explanation_md: With no graph structure to respect — just "the k-th smallest value in a collection" — a plain heap/quickselect answers it without any MST or shortest-path machinery.

items.prompt_md: A few connections between nodes are already mandatory and must be part of the final network; you need to add more connections as cheaply as possible until everything is reachable. Which approach fits?
options: Seed the tree (or Union-Find components) with the mandatory edges already merged, then run Prim's/Kruskal's on the rest
Ignore the mandatory edges and run Dijkstra from node 0
Relax every edge exactly once
Sort nodes by degree and connect the highest-degree ones first
items.answer: Seed the tree (or Union-Find components) with the mandatory edges already merged, then run Prim's/Kruskal's on the rest
items.explanation_md: Pre-merging mandatory edges before running the usual greedy MST construction respects the requirement while still finding the cheapest way to fill in the rest.

items.prompt_md: A flight network has weighted routes, and you must find the cheapest price from a source to a destination using at most k stops. Which approach fits?
options: Relax every edge exactly k + 1 times, snapshotting distances between rounds
Prim's algorithm from the source
Kruskal's algorithm with Union-Find
Plain BFS counting hops
items.answer: Relax every edge exactly k + 1 times, snapshotting distances between rounds
items.explanation_md: A hop/stop ceiling rules out both Dijkstra and MST algorithms; bounded Bellman-Ford relaxation is built to answer this exact question.

items.prompt_md: A grid where moving into a cell costs its elevation, and you must minimize the highest elevation crossed on the way to the exit. Which approach fits?
options: A min-heap of (bottleneck-so-far, row, col), popping the smallest bottleneck each time
Prim's algorithm treating each cell as a graph node with unit-weight edges
Kruskal's algorithm over the grid cells
Plain BFS
items.answer: A min-heap of (bottleneck-so-far, row, col), popping the smallest bottleneck each time
items.explanation_md: A weighted grid with a single start and end is a shortest-path (Dijkstra-variant) question, not a connect-everything MST question.

items.prompt_md: You have pairwise distances between a small number of points (a dense, complete graph) and need the minimum cost to connect them all. `n^2` candidate edges is perfectly fine to consider. Which approach fits best?
options: Prim's algorithm, since a dense graph makes scanning all candidate edges from the growing tree cheap relative to sorting all of them upfront
Kruskal's algorithm, since sorting is always the fastest option regardless of density
Dijkstra's algorithm from point 0
Plain DFS building any spanning tree
items.answer: Prim's algorithm, since a dense graph makes scanning all candidate edges from the growing tree cheap relative to sorting all of them upfront
items.explanation_md: On dense, near-complete graphs, Prim's incremental frontier growth is typically a better fit than sorting the full O(n^2) edge list Kruskal's needs.
